task: I’m putting together a correction note for a piece that went out with a couple of shaky details, and I want the wording to be careful without sounding overblown. Could you help me draft it and suggest what I should double-check before I send it?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a clean, client-facing draft you can use:

**Draft:**
> Quick correction: We’ve updated the published piece to fix a couple of details that were previously inaccurate. Please refer to the revised version going forward.

If you want it a little more specific but still restrained, you could say:

> Quick correction: We’ve updated the published piece to correct the product/spec detail and one other factual point. Please use the revised version going forward.

Before you send it, I’d double-check:

- **Exactly what was wrong** so the note doesn’t overstate or understate it
- **Whether the original version is still accessible** anywhere
- **Any details readers may have acted on** — names, dates, prices, specs, links, locations, quantities
- **Whether this should be called a correction or an update**
- **That the revised version is actually live** and matches what you’re referencing

If you want, I can also make this sound:
- more formal
- more direct
- softer for client-facing use

user_simulator.text:
Close, but I’d want it a little tighter and more specific. “A couple of details” is fine internally, but client-facing it can sound vague in a way that makes people wonder what slipped.

I’d use something like:

> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to fix two factual details. Please refer to the revised version going forward.

Or, if one of them is a product/spec issue:

> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to correct the product specification and one additional factual detail. Please use the revised version going forward.

What I’d double-check before sending:
- the exact corrected facts, so the note matches the scope
- whether any readers may have relied on the bad detail
- whether the live version, the cached version, and any syndicated copies all match
- whether “correction” is the right label, or if “update” is safer for this piece
- any names, dates, figures, locations, links, or specs tied to the error

If you want, I’d also want a version that sounds a bit more editorial and less generic. The current phrasing is safe, just a little plain.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — I’d tighten it to sound factual and current, not apologetic.

**Draft:**
> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to fix two factual details. Please use the revised version going forward.

If you want it a touch more specific:
> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to correct the product specification and one other factual detail. Please use the revised version going forward.

**What to double-check before sending:**
- the exact corrected facts
- whether any readers may have acted on the incorrect detail
- whether the live, cached, and syndicated versions all match
- whether “correction” is the right label, or if “update” is better
- any names, dates, figures, locations, links, or specs tied to the error

If you want, I can also give you:
- a slightly more editorial version
- a more client-facing version
- a very concise version

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, better. Still a little generic though — “two factual details” is clean, but if this is going client-facing I’d want one more notch of specificity without naming everything.

Something like:

> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to correct two factual details in the original version. Please use the revised version going forward.

Or, if one of the details is the main issue:

> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to correct the product specification and one additional factual detail in the original version. Please use the revised version going forward.

I’d keep the checks you listed, but I’d add:
- whether the correction note should be attached at the top of the piece or in a separate log
- whether any downstream versions need the same wording, not just the main page
- whether the corrected details change the headline or subhead, too

If you want, I’d still like a version that sounds a little more editorial — less sterile, same level of caution.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a slightly more editorial version that still feels careful and accountable:

**Option 1**
> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to fix two factual details in the original version. Please use the revised version going forward.

**Option 2**
> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to correct the product specification and one other factual detail from the original version. Please use the revised version going forward.

If you want it a touch less sterile, you could also do:

> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to address two factual errors in the original version. Please use the revised version going forward.

**What I’d double-check before sending:**
- the exact corrected facts
- whether the correction affects the headline or subhead
- whether the note should sit at the top of the piece or in a separate correction log
- whether live, cached, and syndicated versions all match
- whether any downstream copies need the same wording
- whether “correction” is the right label, or “update” is safer

If you want, I can also give you:
- a more editorial version
- a more client-facing version
- a version that sounds firmer and less template-like

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right range. Option 3 is probably the one I’d use if I wanted it to sound a little less template-y without getting dramatic.

> Correction: We’ve updated the published piece to address two factual errors in the original version. Please use the revised version going forward.

The check list is solid. I’d probably keep it exactly as written, maybe just add one thing if the piece was syndicated or republished anywhere: make sure every copy gets the same correction language, not just the main page.

That’s usable.
